summary: Before the Garden, before anything, the texts agree on the starting scene: water and darkness — a formless deep — and a power that divides it. Light from dark, sky from sea, dry land from the waters. Creation, told from Israel to Babylon to Egypt to India, is an act of separation: order called up out of chaos.

sources.provenance: The opening of the Hebrew Bible.
sources.quote: And the earth was a formless and desolate emptiness, and darkness was over the surface of the deep, and the Spirit of God was hovering over the surface of the waters. Then God said, “Let there be light”; and there was light.
sources.translation: New American Standard Bible (NASB), © The Lockman Foundation. Used by permission.
sources.notes: The pattern in three verses: a watery formless deep, darkness, a dividing word, and first light.

sources.provenance: Babylon's creation epic, recovered at Nineveh.
sources.quote: When in the height heaven was not named, and the earth beneath did not yet bear a name, and the primeval Apsû, who begat them, and chaos, Tiamat, the mother of them both — their waters were mingled together, and no field was formed, no marsh was to be seen.
sources.citation: Enuma Elish, Tablet I.1–6, trans. L. W. King (1902).
sources.notes: It begins with only the mingled waters; the storm-god Marduk later splits the sea-goddess Tiamat to make the sky above and the earth below — sky divided from the waters, as in Genesis.

sources.work: The waters of Nun; the Pyramid Texts
sources.quote: In the beginning there is only Nun, the dark and boundless primeval ocean. Out of it rises the first mound of dry land, and upon it the creator Atum brings himself into being and begins to make the ordered world.
sources.translation: Summarized from the Heliopolitan creation (Pyramid Texts).
sources.citation: Pyramid Texts; Heliopolitan cosmogony (Nun, Atum, the Benben).
sources.notes: Egypt also begins with a dark watery deep (Nun) and dry land rising out of it — the same opening image, written down over four thousand years ago.

sources.quote: Then was not non-existent nor existent: there was no realm of air, no sky beyond it. … Darkness there was: at first concealed in darkness this All was indiscriminated chaos. All that existed then was void and formless.
sources.translation: Ralph T. H. Griffith (1896), public domain.
sources.citation: Rigveda 10.129.1–3, trans. R. T. H. Griffith (1896).
sources.notes: India's oldest scripture opens on the very same scene: darkness, a formless void, undivided waters — before anything was named.

sources.quote: Of old was the age when Ymir lived; sea nor cool waves nor sand there were; earth had not been, nor heaven above, but a yawning gap, and grass nowhere.
sources.citation: Völuspá 3 (Poetic Edda), trans. H. A. Bellows (1923).
sources.notes: The far north remembers it as Ginnungagap, the great yawning void before earth or sky — the same primeval formlessness.

sources.quote: In the beginning all was undivided chaos, shaped like an egg. Within it Pangu grew, and when he broke forth he separated the heavens above from the earth below, holding them apart so the ordered world could exist.
sources.notes: Even in China: undivided chaos, then heaven separated from earth — the dividing of the world is the act of creation again.

sources.quote: The Hermetic creation opens like Genesis: out of a watery darkness comes a holy Word and a Light, and by that Word the formless, moist nature is ordered into the cosmos. 'That Light,' it says, 'is the Mind, the first God' — and His Word moves upon the deep.
sources.translation: After G. R. S. Mead (1906), public domain.
sources.notes: Egypt's philosophical tradition, attributed to 'thrice-great Hermes,' tells creation as a Word and a Light bringing order out of a watery dark — the same picture as Genesis 1, from a different people.

science.intro: The texts say it started: a beginning, a first light, order drawn out of formless dark. For most of history science assumed an eternal, unchanging universe. The modern evidence flipped to the texts' side — the cosmos had a beginning, and it began with light.

evidence.title: The universe had a beginning
evidence.observation: The cosmos is expanding from an initial event ~13.8 billion years ago — it is not eternal and unchanging, as scientists long assumed; it had a starting point.
evidence.tie: The texts open with a beginning, not an eternal world. Science was dragged to the same conclusion against its own preference.

evidence.title: 'Let there be light'
evidence.observation: The whole sky glows faintly with the cosmic microwave background — the leftover light from the universe's first moments, the oldest light there is.
evidence.tie: A first light at the foundation of everything, still detectable. The text names it first too.

evidence.title: A cosmos tuned for life
evidence.observation: The fundamental constants of physics fall in extraordinarily narrow ranges that permit stars, chemistry and life; small changes and nothing could exist.
evidence.tie: Order finely set, not random chaos — what you'd expect of a creation, not an accident.

evidence.title: All life runs on one code
evidence.observation: Every living thing on earth, from bacteria to humans, shares the same DNA/RNA genetic code and core biochemistry — pointing to a single origin of life.
evidence.tie: One source for all living things, as the texts say.
